title: Arts and Culture: Theatre, Art, Architecture, Literature
overview: British theatre, art, architecture, and literature have had enormous influence on world culture. From Shakespeare's plays to the Gothic revival in architecture, from the Pre-Raphaelites to modern contemporary art, Britain has produced remarkable cultural achievements. This lesson covers the development of British theatre from the West End to the Edinburgh Fringe, notable British artists from Gainsborough to Hockney, architects from Wren to modern masters, and the prestigious literary tradition including major authors and the Man Booker Prize.
key_facts: There are theatres in most towns and cities throughout the UK, ranging from large to small, showing both professional and amateur productions.
London's West End, also known as 'Theatreland', is particularly well known for theatre.
The Mousetrap, a murder-mystery play by Dame Agatha Christie, has been running in the West End since 1952.
The Mousetrap has had the longest initial run of any show in history.
In the 19th century, Gilbert and Sullivan wrote comic operas, often making fun of popular culture and politics.
Gilbert and Sullivan's operas include HMS Pinafore, The Pirates of Penzance and The Mikado.
Gilbert and Sullivan's work is still often staged by professional and amateur groups.
Andrew Lloyd Webber has written music for shows popular throughout the world.
Andrew Lloyd Webber collaborated with Tim Rice on Jesus Christ Superstar and Evita.
Andrew Lloyd Webber also wrote Cats and The Phantom of the Opera.
Pantomime is a British tradition produced by many theatres at Christmas time.
Pantomimes are based on fairy stories and are light-hearted plays with music and comedy, enjoyed by family audiences.
One traditional pantomime character is the Dame, a woman played by a man.
There is often also a pantomime horse or cow played by two actors in the same costume.
The Edinburgh Festival takes place in Edinburgh, Scotland, every summer.
The Edinburgh Festival is a series of different arts and cultural festivals.
The biggest and most well-known Edinburgh festival is the Edinburgh Festival Fringe (the Fringe).
The Fringe is a showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances and often shows experimental work.
The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London.
The Olivier Awards have categories including best director, best actor and best actress.
The awards are named after the British actor Sir Laurence Olivier, later Lord Olivier, who was best known for his roles in various Shakespeare plays.
During the Middle Ages, most art had a religious theme, particularly wall paintings in churches and illustrations in religious books.
Much medieval art was lost after the Protestant Reformation but wealthy families began to collect other paintings and sculptures.
Many painters working in Britain in the 16th and 17th centuries were from abroad, for example Hans Holbein and Sir Anthony Van Dyck.
British artists, particularly those painting portraits and landscapes, became well known from the 18th century onwards.
Works by British and international artists are displayed in galleries across the UK.
Well-known galleries include The National Gallery, Tate Britain and Tate Modern in London, the National Museum in Cardiff, and the National Gallery of Scotland in Edinburgh.
The Turner Prize was established in 1984 and celebrates contemporary art.
The Turner Prize was named after Joseph Turner.
Four works are shortlisted every year for the Turner Prize and shown at Tate Britain before the winner is announced.
The Turner Prize is recognised as one of the most prestigious visual art awards in Europe.
Previous Turner Prize winners include Damien Hirst and Richard Wright.
Thomas Gainsborough (1727-88) was a portrait painter who often painted people in country or garden scenery.
David Allan (1744-96) was a Scottish painter best known for painting portraits. One of his most famous works is The Origin of Painting.
Joseph Turner (1775-1851) was an influential landscape painter in a modern style. He is considered the artist who raised the profile of landscape painting.
John Constable (1776-1837) was a landscape painter most famous for his works of Dedham Vale on the Suffolk-Essex border in the east of England.
The Pre-Raphaelites were an important group of artists in the second half of the 19th century.
The Pre-Raphaelites painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours.
The Pre-Raphaelite group included Holman Hunt, Dante Gabriel Rossetti and Sir John Millais.
Sir John Lavery (1856-1941) was a very successful Northern Irish portrait painter whose work included painting the Royal Family.
Henry Moore (1898-1986) was an English sculptor and artist best known for his large bronze abstract sculptures.
John Petts (1914-91) was a Welsh artist, best known for his engravings and stained glass.
Lucian Freud (1922-2011) was a German-born British artist best known for his portraits.
David Hockney (1937-) was an important contributor to the 'pop art' movement of the 1960s and continues to be influential today.
The architectural heritage of the UK is rich and varied.
In the Middle Ages, great cathedrals and churches were built, many of which still stand today.
Examples of medieval cathedrals include those in Durham, Lincoln, Canterbury and Salisbury.
The White Tower in the Tower of London is an example of a Norman castle keep, built on the orders of William the Conqueror.
As the countryside became more peaceful and landowners richer, great country houses such as Hardwick Hall in Derbyshire were built.
In the 17th century, Inigo Jones took inspiration from classical architecture to design the Queen's House at Greenwich and the Banqueting House in Whitehall in London.
Sir Christopher Wren helped develop a British version of ornate styles popular in Europe in buildings such as the new St Paul's Cathedral.
In the 18th century, simpler designs became popular.
The Scottish architect Robert Adam influenced the development of architecture in the UK, Europe and America.
Robert Adam designed the inside decoration as well as the building itself in great houses such as Dumfries House in Scotland.
Robert Adam's ideas influenced architects in cities such as Bath, where the Royal Crescent was built.
In the 19th century, the medieval 'gothic' style became popular again.
As cities expanded in the 19th century, many great public buildings were built in gothic style.
The Houses of Parliament and St Pancras Station were built in the 19th century, as were town halls in cities such as Manchester and Sheffield.
In the 20th century, Sir Edwin Lutyens had an influence throughout the British Empire.
Sir Edwin Lutyens designed New Delhi to be the seat of government in India.
After the First World War, Lutyens was responsible for many war memorials throughout the world, including the Cenotaph in Whitehall.
The Cenotaph is the site of the annual Remembrance Day service attended by the Queen, politicians and foreign ambassadors.
Modern British architects including Sir Norman Foster, Lord (Richard) Rogers and Dame Zaha Hadid continue to work on major projects throughout the world.
Garden design and landscaping have played an important role in the UK alongside architecture.
In the 18th century, Lancelot 'Capability' Brown designed the grounds around country houses so the landscape appeared natural, with grass, trees and lakes.
Capability Brown often said that a place had 'capabilities'.
Later, Gertrude Jekyll often worked with Edwin Lutyens to design colourful gardens around the houses he designed.
Gardens continue to be an important part of homes in the UK.
The annual Chelsea Flower Show showcases garden design from Britain and around the world.

Britain has produced many great designers, from Thomas Chippendale (who designed furniture in the 18th century) to Clarice Cliff (who designed Art Deco ceramics) to Sir Terence Conran (a 20th-century interior designer).
Leading fashion designers of recent years include Mary Quant, Alexander McQueen and Vivienne Westwood.
The UK has a prestigious literary history and tradition.
Several British writers have won the Nobel Prize in Literature, including the novelist Sir William Golding, the poet Seamus Heaney, and the playwright Harold Pinter.
Other authors have become well known in popular fiction, such as Agatha Christie (detective stories) and Ian Fleming (James Bond books).
In 2003, The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted the country's best-loved novel.
The Man Booker Prize for Fiction is awarded annually for the best fiction novel written by an author from the Commonwealth, Ireland or Zimbabwe.
The Man Booker Prize has been awarded since 1968.
Past Man Booker Prize winners include Ian McEwan, Hilary Mantel and Julian Barnes.
Jane Austen (1775-1817) was an English novelist. Her books include Pride and Prejudice and Sense and Sensibility.
Jane Austen's novels are concerned with marriage and family relationships. Many have been made into television programmes or films.
Charles Dickens (1812-70) wrote very famous novels including Oliver Twist and Great Expectations.
You will hear references in everyday talk to some Dickens characters, such as Scrooge (a mean person) or Mr Micawber (always hopeful).
Robert Louis Stevenson (1850-94) wrote books still read by adults and children today, including Treasure Island, Kidnapped and Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde.
Thomas Hardy (1840-1928) was an author and poet. His best-known novels focus on rural society and include Far from the Madding Crowd and Jude the Obscure.
Sir Arthur Conan Doyle (1859-1930) was a Scottish doctor and writer best known for his stories about Sherlock Holmes, one of the first fictional detectives.
Evelyn Waugh (1903-66) wrote satirical novels including Decline and Fall and Scoop. He is perhaps best known for Brideshead Revisited.
Sir Kingsley Amis (1922-95) was an English novelist and poet who wrote more than 20 novels. The most well-known is Lucky Jim.
Graham Greene (1904-91) wrote novels often influenced by his religious beliefs, including The Heart of the Matter, The Honorary Consul, Brighton Rock and Our Man in Havana.
J K Rowling (1965-) wrote the Harry Potter series of children's books, which have enjoyed huge international success. She now writes fiction for adults as well.
memory_hook: Imagine a grand cultural tour: Start at the West End where The Mousetrap has run since 1952, then visit Tate Britain to see Turner Prize winners and Pre-Raphaelites painting in bright colours. Walk through Bath's Royal Crescent (Robert Adam's influence), then to St Paul's Cathedral (Christopher Wren's masterpiece). Stop at the Cenotaph (Lutyens' war memorial) for Remembrance Day. Visit Chelsea Flower Show (Gertrude Jekyll's legacy), then curl up with Pride and Prejudice (Jane Austen), Oliver Twist (Dickens), Sherlock Holmes (Conan Doyle), and Harry Potter (J K Rowling). End at Edinburgh Fringe for experimental theatre. From medieval cathedrals to modern architecture, from Thomas Gainsborough's portraits to David Hockney's pop art, Britain's cultural treasures span centuries.

questions.prompt: Which play by Dame Agatha Christie has been running in the West End since 1952?
questions.explanation: The Mousetrap has been running in the West End since 1952 and has had the longest initial run of any show in history.

questions.prompt: What is the Turner Prize awarded for?
questions.explanation: The Turner Prize, established in 1984 and named after Joseph Turner, celebrates contemporary art. It is one of the most prestigious visual art awards in Europe.
Sir Christopher Wren helped develop a British version of ornate styles popular in Europe in buildings such as the new St Paul's Cathedral.

questions.prompt: What is the Edinburgh Festival Fringe known for?
options: Mainly theatre and comedy performances, often experimental
questions.explanation: The Fringe is the biggest Edinburgh festival and is a showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances. It often shows experimental work.

questions.prompt: Who wrote the Harry Potter series?
questions.explanation: J K Rowling wrote the Harry Potter series of children's books, which have enjoyed huge international success.

questions.prompt: What is a traditional British Christmas theatre show called?
questions.explanation: Pantomime is a British tradition. Many theatres produce a pantomime at Christmas time based on fairy stories with music and comedy.

options: An important group of 19th century artists who painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours
questions.explanation: The Pre-Raphaelites were an important group of artists in the second half of the 19th century who painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours. The group included Holman Hunt, Dante Gabriel Rossetti and Sir John Millais.

questions.prompt: Which architect designed the Cenotaph war memorial in Whitehall?
questions.explanation: Sir Edwin Lutyens was responsible for many war memorials after the First World War, including the Cenotaph in Whitehall, which is the site of the annual Remembrance Day service.

questions.prompt: What prize is awarded annually for the best fiction novel from the Commonwealth, Ireland or Zimbabwe?
questions.explanation: The Man Booker Prize for Fiction is awarded annually for the best fiction novel written by an author from the Commonwealth, Ireland or Zimbabwe. It has been awarded since 1968.
Jane Austen (1775-1817) wrote Pride and Prejudice and Sense and Sensibility. Her novels are concerned with marriage and family relationships.
